meta.source: Rob Walton Foundation / African Parks / WCS / Frankfurt Zoological Society
meta.paper: Keystone protected areas are vitally important for nature conservation and socioeconomic opportunity in Africa

keystones.name: Cameia
keystones.description: A vast wetland and miombo woodland park in central Angola, centred on Lake Cameia and the Zambezi headwaters. The park supports hippos, sitatunga, and large waterbird colonies, and is one of the most significant freshwater ecosystems in southern Angola.
keystones.manager: Instituto Nacional da Biodiversidade e Areas de Conservacao

keystones.name: Iona
keystones.description: Angola largest national park, encompassing dramatic desert landscapes where the Namib meets the Atlantic coast, supporting endemic plants, desert-adapted elephants, and rare coastal wildlife in one of the least disturbed arid ecosystems on Earth.

keystones.name: Luando
keystones.description: An integral nature reserve in central Angola protecting one of the last strongholds of the giant sable antelope, Angola's national animal. Dense miombo woodland and riverine corridors define the landscape, which remains among the least disturbed in the region.
keystones.manager: Instituto Nacional da Biodiversidade e Areas de Conservacao

keystones.name: Luengue-Luiana
keystones.description: One of Angola's largest protected areas, spanning vast miombo woodlands and wetlands along the Cuando and Luiana rivers in the southeast. Managed by African Parks since 2018, it is being restored after decades of civil war, with elephant and buffalo populations recovering steadily.

keystones.name: Pendjari
keystones.description: A West African savanna stronghold forming part of the WAP transboundary complex, sheltering 90% of the remaining West African lion population alongside elephants, hippos, and critically endangered West African cheetah.

keystones.name: W National Park
keystones.description: The Benin sector of the UNESCO-listed WAP transboundary complex, protecting diverse Guinea-Sudanian savanna ecosystems and forming a critical corridor for the last viable lion, elephant, and buffalo populations in West Africa.

keystones.name: Central Kalahari
keystones.description: One of the largest protected areas in the world and the ancestral homeland of the San Bushmen, protecting a vast Kalahari ecosystem where wild dog, brown hyena, and desert-adapted lion range across fossil riverbeds and salt pans.

keystones.name: Chobe
keystones.description: Botswana's first national park and one of Africa's most celebrated wildlife destinations, home to the continent's highest concentration of elephants. The Chobe River floodplain draws enormous herds during the dry season, supporting lions, leopards, wild dogs, and exceptional birdlife.
keystones.manager: Department of Wildlife and National Parks Botswana

keystones.name: Gemsbok
keystones.description: The Botswana portion of the Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park, one of the world's great desert conservation areas straddling the Kalahari. Gemsbok, springbok, lions, and black-maned Kalahari lions roam ancient fossilised riverbeds in one of Africa's largest unfenced protected areas.
keystones.manager: South African National Parks / Department of Wildlife and National Parks Botswana

keystones.name: Bamingui-Bangoran
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage component in northern CAR protecting savanna and gallery forest ecosystems at the Sahel-Sudan transition zone. The park harbours elephants, lions, leopards, and cheetahs, though populations have been severely impacted by poaching and armed conflict.

keystones.name: Chinko
keystones.description: A remote and vast protected area in southeastern CAR, recovering under African Parks management after decades of civil conflict left it empty of wildlife, now showing signs of elephant and giant eland recovery in one of Africa most inaccessible landscapes.

keystones.name: Dzanga-Sangha
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage rainforest reserve in the southwest CAR, famous for the Dzanga bai forest clearing where hundreds of forest elephants gather to drink mineral-rich water. The reserve protects western lowland gorillas, bongo, and one of the most biodiverse forest ecosystems in Central Africa.
keystones.manager: WWF / Government of Central African Republic

keystones.name: Manovo-Gounda St Floris
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage site in northern CAR encompassing sweeping savanna, wetlands, and gallery forests along the Bahr Aouk and Bahr Kameur rivers. Once supporting exceptional wildlife including black rhinos, the park is listed as World Heritage in Danger due to poaching and armed conflict.

keystones.name: Comoe
keystones.description: Ivory Coast's largest protected area and a UNESCO World Heritage site, spanning a transition zone from Sahel to rainforest along the Comoe River. The park supports unusual ecological diversity including forest patches within savanna, harbouring chimpanzees, elephants, lions, and over 500 bird species.
keystones.manager: Office Ivoirien des Parcs et Reserves

keystones.name: Tai
keystones.description: The last remaining large tract of primary rainforest in West Africa and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, protecting chimpanzees known for their tool-using behaviour, pygmy hippopotamus, and over 1,000 plant species, many found nowhere else.

keystones.name: Bouba Ndjida
keystones.description: A national park in northern Cameroon along the Cameroon-Chad border, part of the Cameroon Highlands savanna ecosystem. The park has suffered significant elephant losses to poaching but retains populations of lions, giraffes, and roan antelope in its broad floodplains and woodland savannas.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Boumba Bek
keystones.description: A dense rainforest national park in southeastern Cameroon within the Congo Basin forest complex, adjoining Nki National Park. The park protects western lowland gorillas, forest elephants, and chimpanzees in a largely intact forest landscape that forms part of the Dja-Odzala-Minkebe transfrontier complex.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Boumba-Djombi
keystones.description: A proposed protected area in southeastern Cameroon forming a wildlife corridor within the Congo Basin rainforest. The zone links Boumba Bek and Nki national parks and is critical for the movement of forest elephants and great apes across the broader Dja ecosystem.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Campo-Ma'an
keystones.description: A coastal rainforest national park in southwestern Cameroon, encompassing the transition between Atlantic coastal forest and the Congo Basin. The park protects chimpanzees, forest elephants, and African grey parrots, with mangrove systems and offshore islands adding exceptional marine biodiversity.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Dja
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Africa largest and most pristine rainforest reserves, encircled by the Dja River and sheltering western lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, forest elephants, and bongo in an almost entirely intact ecosystem.

keystones.name: Faro
keystones.description: A national park in the Adamawa highlands of northern Cameroon, protecting Guinea savanna and gallery forest habitats along the Faro River. The park supports elephants, lions, hippopotamuses, and diverse antelope species, and is contiguous with the Benoue protected area complex.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Lobeke
keystones.description: A Congo Basin rainforest park in southeast Cameroon, renowned for its network of forest clearings (bais) where western lowland gorillas, forest elephants, and bongo congregate. Lobeke forms part of the Sangha Trinational UNESCO World Heritage site alongside Dzanga-Sangha and Nouabale-Ndoki.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Mbam et Djerem
keystones.description: Cameroon's largest national park, straddling the transition between the Congo Basin rainforest and the Guinea savanna in the country's centre. The park protects a mosaic of habitats supporting western lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, elephants, and bongos in a largely roadless wilderness.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Mont Cameroun
keystones.description: West Africa's highest peak at 4,095m, an active volcano on the Gulf of Guinea coast whose slopes pass through montane forest, sub-alpine heathland, and lava fields. The mountain is a centre of plant endemism and supports forest elephants, chimpanzees, and drill monkeys in its lower forest zones.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Nki
keystones.description: A remote rainforest national park in the southeastern corner of Cameroon, part of the Dja-Odzala-Minkebe transfrontier conservation complex. Nki protects one of the least disturbed forest areas in Central Africa, with healthy populations of gorillas, forest elephants, and chimpanzees.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Takamanda
keystones.description: A small but critically important rainforest park on the Cameroon-Nigeria border, home to one of the last populations of the Cross River gorilla, the world's most endangered great ape subspecies. The park connects with Nigeria's Cross River National Park, forming a vital transboundary refuge.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Waza
keystones.description: A UNESCO biosphere reserve in northern Cameroon at the southern edge of the Sahara, protecting Sahelian savanna and floodplain habitats around Lake Chad. Waza is renowned for its lion population and extraordinary concentrations of waterbirds, giraffes, and elephants during the wet season.
keystones.manager: Ministry of Forestry and Wildlife Cameroon

keystones.name: Abumonbazi
keystones.description: A managed resource protected area in northwestern DRC within the Congo Basin forest, protecting a mosaic of lowland rainforest and swamp forest. The area supports forest elephants, bonobos, and diverse primate communities in one of the world's largest contiguous tropical forest blocks.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature

keystones.name: Bili-Uere
keystones.description: A vast and remote protected hunting domain in northern DRC, famous for a population of unusually large chimpanzees known as Bili apes that display behaviours intermediate between chimpanzees and gorillas. The area encompasses forest-savanna mosaic and is one of the least-studied large protected areas in Africa.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature

keystones.name: Garamba
keystones.description: One of Africa oldest national parks and the last stronghold of the northeastern white rhinoceros, now managed by African Parks since 2005 in a sustained effort to protect its unique savanna-forest mosaic from poaching and armed conflict.

keystones.name: Itombwe
keystones.description: A high-altitude nature reserve in eastern DRC protecting the Itombwe Massif, one of Africa's most important montane forest blocks. The massif is a centre of exceptional endemism for birds, amphibians, and plants, and shelters the world's largest population of Grauer's gorillas alongside chimpanzees and forest elephants.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature

keystones.name: Kahuzi-Biega
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site protecting the Itombwe highlands and the last stronghold of the eastern lowland gorilla - the largest gorilla subspecies - in a mountain and lowland forest complex that remains critically threatened despite its protected status.

keystones.name: Kundelungu
keystones.description: A national park on the Kundelungu Plateau in southeastern DRC, protecting high-altitude grasslands, miombo woodland, and the dramatic Lofoï waterfall, one of Africa's tallest. The park is part of the Upper Lualaba watershed and harbours roan antelope, buffalo, and significant bird diversity.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature

keystones.name: Lomami
keystones.description: A newly established national park in central DRC managed by African Parks, protecting a vast expanse of intact Congo Basin rainforest around the Lomami River. The park was the discovery site of the lesula monkey, a species new to science identified in 2007, and harbours bonobos and forest elephants.

keystones.name: Maiko
keystones.description: A remote and largely inaccessible national park in northeastern DRC protecting dense Congo Basin rainforest. Maiko is one of only three parks in Africa where okapi, Grauer's gorillas, and forest elephants coexist, making it a globally significant biodiversity stronghold despite serious conservation challenges.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature

keystones.name: Okapis
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage reserve in the Ituri Forest of northeastern DRC, protecting the primary habitat of the okapi, one of Africa's most enigmatic mammals. The reserve covers roughly one fifth of the Ituri Forest and shelters forest elephants, chimpanzees, and over 330 bird species in pristine lowland rainforest.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature / Okapi Conservation Project

keystones.name: Salonga
keystones.description: The largest tropical rainforest national park in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, protecting the heart of the Congo Basin and the only remaining habitat of the bonobo - one of humanity closest relatives - alongside forest elephants and Congo peacock.

keystones.name: Tumba-Lediima
keystones.description: A vast wetland and flooded forest reserve in western DRC adjoining the Republic of Congo border, protecting one of the world's most significant freshwater swamp forest systems. The area is a stronghold for bonobos and supports rare swamp-specialist species in a largely intact landscape.
keystones.manager: Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature / WWF

keystones.name: Upemba
keystones.description: A national park in southeastern DRC encompassing the Upemba wetland system, Lake Upemba, and surrounding miombo woodland, now managed by African Parks. Once harbouring extraordinary wildlife, the park is undergoing intensive restoration after decades of conflict and poaching, with elephant populations beginning to recover.

keystones.name: Virunga
keystones.description: Africa oldest national park and a UNESCO World Heritage Site protecting the Virunga Volcanoes and one of the most biodiverse landscapes on Earth, home to mountain gorillas, okapi, and over 700 bird species across active volcanic terrain.

keystones.name: Conkouati-Douli
keystones.description: A national park on the Atlantic coast of the Republic of Congo, protecting a rare combination of marine, mangrove, lagoon, and rainforest ecosystems. The park harbours nesting leatherback turtles, forest elephants, western lowland gorillas, and chimpanzees in a landscape that spans from ocean to deep forest.
keystones.manager: Government of Republic of Congo / WCS

keystones.name: Lac Tele
keystones.description: A remote community reserve in the northern Congo Basin, centred on Lake Tele and its surrounding swamp forest - one of the most intact freshwater ecosystems in Central Africa. The reserve is famous in folklore as the supposed home of the Mokele-mbembe and genuinely supports forest elephants, bonobos, and vast waterbird colonies.

keystones.name: Lessio-Louna
keystones.description: A wildlife reserve in the Bateke Plateau of the Republic of Congo, known internationally as a reintroduction site for western lowland gorillas by the Aspinall Foundation. The plateau savanna and gallery forest landscape supports reintroduced gorilla groups that have successfully bred in the wild.
keystones.manager: The Aspinall Foundation / Government of Republic of Congo

keystones.name: Lefini
keystones.description: A faunal reserve on the Bateke Plateau north of Brazzaville protecting savanna grasslands, gallery forest, and the Lefini River valley. The reserve supports buffalos, sitatunga, and large primate populations, and is part of the broader Bateke Plateau conservation landscape.

keystones.name: Nouabale-Ndoki
keystones.description: A pristine Congo Basin rainforest national park in northern Republic of Congo, widely regarded as one of the most intact forest ecosystems in Africa. Part of the Sangha Trinational UNESCO World Heritage site, the park protects western lowland gorillas, forest elephants, and chimpanzees in a forest that shows virtually no sign of human disturbance.
keystones.manager: Wildlife Conservation Society / Government of Republic of Congo

keystones.name: Ntokou-Pikounda
keystones.description: A recently established national park in northern Republic of Congo protecting one of the highest-density western lowland gorilla populations ever recorded. The park's swamp forest and terra firma rainforest form a corridor connecting Nouabale-Ndoki and Odzala-Kokoua, cementing a landscape-scale conservation block.
keystones.manager: Wildlife Conservation Society / Government of Republic of Congo

keystones.name: Odzala-Kokoua
keystones.description: One of Africa oldest national parks, protecting a vast expanse of Congo Basin rainforest with one of the highest densities of western lowland gorillas on the planet, alongside forest elephants, bongo, and over 400 bird species.

keystones.name: Ahaggar
keystones.description: A vast national park in southern Algeria encompassing the Ahaggar Mountains, a dramatic volcanic massif rising from the Sahara with peaks exceeding 2,900m. The park protects Saharan endemic flora and fauna including Barbary sheep, sand gazelles, and Saharan cheetahs in one of the world's largest protected areas.
keystones.manager: Office du Parc National de l'Ahaggar

keystones.name: Bale Mountains
keystones.description: The highest peak in the southern Ethiopian highlands and the global stronghold of the Ethiopian wolf - the world most endangered canid - alongside mountain nyala and Bale monkey in a dramatic Afroalpine ecosystem.

keystones.name: Borana
keystones.description: A private conservancy in northern Kenya's Laikipia Plateau, part of the broader Laikipia wildlife ecosystem that supports the second-largest wildlife population in Kenya outside the Mara-Serengeti. Borana has been instrumental in the translocation and recovery of critically endangered black rhinos and Grevy's zebras.

keystones.name: Gambella
keystones.description: Ethiopias largest national park and the southern anchor of the Great Nile Migration, added to the African Parks portfolio in 2024 to form a seamless management continuum with Boma and Badingilo across the South Sudan border.

keystones.name: Ivindo
keystones.description: A national park in northeast Gabon protecting dense Congo Basin rainforest and the Ivindo River, home to the spectacular Kongou and Mingouli waterfalls. The park harbours large populations of forest elephants, western lowland gorillas, and chimpanzees, and is considered one of Gabon's most biologically rich landscapes.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Loango
keystones.description: A remarkable coastal national park in western Gabon where rainforest meets the Atlantic Ocean, creating a mosaic of beach, lagoon, savanna, and forest. Loango is famous for surfing hippos, forest elephants on the beach, and nesting sea turtles, earning its reputation as Africa's last Eden.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Lope
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site at the transition zone between equatorial rainforest and savanna, one of the most scientifically studied ecosystems in Central Africa, sheltering forest elephants, western gorillas, and over 1,500 plant species.

keystones.name: Minkebe
keystones.description: A remote and vast rainforest in northern Gabon, one of the last truly intact forest elephant strongholds in the Congo Basin, accessible only by helicopter and protecting extraordinary concentrations of megafauna largely undisturbed by human activity.

keystones.name: Monts Birougou
keystones.description: A montane forest national park in southern Gabon protecting the Birougou Hills, an isolated massif with high plant endemism and diverse wildlife. The park shelters western lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, and forest elephants within a forest that remains largely unexplored by scientists.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Monts de Cristal
keystones.description: A national park in northwestern Gabon protecting the Crystal Mountains, a Pre-Cambrian highland with exceptional botanical diversity and one of the highest levels of plant endemism in Central Africa. The park is critical habitat for mandrills, western lowland gorillas, and numerous endemic bird species.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Moukalaba-Doudou
keystones.description: A national park in southwestern Gabon protecting lowland rainforest, savanna, and mangrove systems along the Atlantic coast. The park is known for habituated western lowland gorilla groups and forest elephants, and forms part of a broader landscape connecting to Conkouati-Douli in the Republic of Congo.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Plateaux Batek
keystones.description: A national park on the Bateke Plateau in southeastern Gabon protecting savanna, gallery forest, and intact rainforest at the Congo Basin fringe. The park is an important refuge for western lowland gorillas and forest elephants within a landscape that has faced pressure from agriculture and logging.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Wonga-Wongue
keystones.description: A presidential reserve on Gabon's Atlantic coast protecting coastal forest, wetlands, and lagoons with exceptional biodiversity. The reserve serves as a personal hunting ground historically and remains one of the best-preserved coastal ecosystems in Central Africa, with large forest elephant herds and nesting sea turtles.
keystones.manager: Agence Nationale des Parcs Nationaux Gabon

keystones.name: Mole
keystones.description: Ghana's largest national park, protecting Guinea savanna woodland and gallery forest in the northwest of the country. Mole is home to Ghana's largest elephant population alongside lions, leopards, kobs, and over 300 bird species, and represents the most significant wildlife area in West Africa's drier savanna zone.

keystones.name: Monte Alen
keystones.description: The largest protected area on Equatorial Guinea's mainland, Bioko, protecting dense equatorial rainforest on the slopes of Monte Mitra and Monte Alen. The park harbours western lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, and forest elephants in one of Central Africa's lesser-known but biologically significant forest landscapes.

keystones.name: Reserva de la Paz
keystones.description: A biosphere reserve on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, protecting montane forest and cloud forest habitats on the slopes of an extinct volcano. The island is globally significant for endemic primates including the drill and Bioko red colobus, and hosts important nesting populations of sea turtles on its southern beaches.

keystones.name: Aberdare
keystones.description: A national park protecting the Aberdare Mountain Range in central Kenya, encompassing bamboo forest, moorland, and the Kinangop Plateau above 3,000m. The park forms part of the Central Kenya corridor for elephants and black rhinos, and is famous for its tree hotels where wildlife congregates at waterholes.

keystones.name: Masai Mara
keystones.description: The Kenyan sector of the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem and the most celebrated wildlife reserve in Africa, famous for the annual wildebeest crossing of the Mara River and its exceptional concentrations of the Big Five year-round.

keystones.name: Melako Community Conservancy
keystones.description: A community-managed conservancy in northern Kenya's Samburu-Isiolo region, established by the Borana community to protect wildlife and secure land rights. The conservancy is a key corridor for elephants and Grevy's zebras and demonstrates the effectiveness of community-led conservation in northern Kenya's drylands.

keystones.name: Mount Kenya
keystones.description: Africa second highest peak and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, protecting glaciers, alpine moorland, and montane forest on an ancient volcanic cone that sustains Kenya water supply and shelters forest elephant, bongo, and black rhino.

keystones.name: Sera Community Conservancy
keystones.description: Kenya's first community conservancy to successfully reintroduce black rhinos, with a rhino sanctuary established in 2015 now holding a growing population. Managed by the Samburu community with support from the Northern Rangelands Trust, Sera is a leading example of community conservation generating both wildlife recovery and livelihood benefits.
keystones.manager: Sera Community Conservancy / Northern Rangelands Trust

keystones.name: Sibiloi
keystones.description: A national park on the northeastern shore of Lake Turkana, a UNESCO World Heritage site and the world's largest permanent desert lake. Sibiloi is globally significant for its fossil record, with discoveries including Homo rudolfensis and Australopithecus anamensis, and protects Nile crocodiles, hippos, and specialist desert fauna.

keystones.name: Tsavo East
keystones.description: One of the world largest wildlife reserves, protecting a semi-arid landscape of red elephant, expansive lava flows, and the Galana River as it cuts through an ancient wilderness that remains remarkably wild and undisturbed.

keystones.name: Tsavo West
keystones.description: The western sector of the great Tsavo ecosystem, featuring volcanic landscapes, underground rivers, and the Ngulia Rhino Sanctuary - one of Kenya most important black rhino refuges - alongside large elephant herds and a diverse bird community.

keystones.name: Sapo
keystones.description: Liberia's only national park and the second-largest protected rainforest in West Africa, sheltering a remarkable assemblage of Upper Guinea forest species. Sapo protects pygmy hippos, forest elephants, chimpanzees, and zebra duikers in an intact primary forest that has survived despite decades of civil conflict.

keystones.name: Maloti-Drakensberg
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site spanning Lesotho and South Africa, protecting the southern African escarpment and its extraordinary endemic flora, ancient San rock art, and the headwaters of major river systems sustaining millions across the region.

keystones.name: Gourma
keystones.description: A Sahelian reserve in eastern Mali protecting the southernmost population of desert-adapted elephants on Earth, which undertake the longest elephant migration recorded in Africa - over 600km annually in search of water. The herd's survival in this harsh landscape represents one of conservation's most extraordinary stories.

keystones.name: Banhine
keystones.description: A remote national park in southern Mozambique's Gaza Province, protecting seasonal pans, grasslands, and woodland in the Limpopo basin. Part of the Great Limpopo Transfrontier Conservation Area, Banhine is being restocked with wildlife and forms a critical link in a vast cross-border ecosystem spanning Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and South Africa.
keystones.manager: Administracao Nacional das Areas de Conservacao Mozambique

keystones.name: Chimanimani
keystones.description: A compact but spectacular national park in eastern Zimbabwe protecting the Chimanimani Mountains, a quartzite massif of great botanical significance on the Mozambique border. The park's diverse habitats from river gorges to high plateaux support sable antelope, leopards, and exceptionally high plant endemism within the Eastern Highlands.
keystones.manager: Zimbabwe Parks and Wildlife Management Authority

keystones.name: Gile
keystones.description: A national reserve in Zambezia Province, Mozambique, protecting miombo woodland and the Molócue River system. Gile holds one of Mozambique's most important elephant populations and is being rehabilitated following years of conflict and poaching, with support from international conservation organisations.
keystones.manager: Administracao Nacional das Areas de Conservacao Mozambique

keystones.name: Gorongosa
keystones.description: One of Africa great wildlife restoration stories, transformed by the Carr Foundation and the Mozambican government from a park devastated by civil war into a thriving ecosystem with recovering buffalo, lion, elephant, and hippo populations.

keystones.name: Limpopo
keystones.description: The Mozambican component of the Great Limpopo Transfrontier Park, connecting to South Africa's Kruger National Park and Zimbabwe's Gonarezhou. Fences between Limpopo and Kruger have been removed, allowing elephants and lions to roam freely across the international border in one of Africa's largest conservation areas.
keystones.manager: Administracao Nacional das Areas de Conservacao Mozambique

keystones.name: Niassa
keystones.description: One of the largest protected areas in Africa and arguably the continent most important wilderness frontier, sheltering one of Africa largest remaining elephant populations in a remote miombo ecosystem the size of Switzerland.

keystones.name: Tchuma Tchato
keystones.description: A community-based multiple use area on the Zambezi River in Tete Province, Mozambique, one of Africa's pioneering community conservation projects since the 1990s. Local communities share in wildlife revenues and manage resource use, with hippos, crocodiles, and diverse waterbirds in the Zambezi corridor.

keystones.name: Zinave
keystones.description: A national park in southern Mozambique managed by African Parks since 2017, undergoing comprehensive wildlife restoration after being emptied of large mammals during the civil war. Lions, elephants, hippos, nyala, and wild dogs have been reintroduced and populations are growing in this expanding Great Limpopo landscape.

keystones.name: Liwonde
keystones.description: Malawi flagship wildlife destination, transformed by African Parks from a heavily poached reserve to a thriving Big Five ecosystem on the Shire River, including the translocation of black rhino and hundreds of elephants to rewild the landscape.

keystones.name: Mulanje Mountain
keystones.description: A forest reserve protecting the Mulanje Massif in southern Malawi, an inselberg rising dramatically to 3,002m - the highest point in south-central Africa. The mountain is a centre of plant endemism including the Mulanje cedar, Malawi's national tree, and supports endemic chameleons, birds, and a rich montane forest community.

keystones.name: Nyika
keystones.description: Malawi highest plateau and a biodiversity hotspot for montane flora and large mammals, sheltering roan antelope, eland, leopard, and zebra across rolling grasslands that give way to evergreen forest in a landscape unlike anywhere else in the region.

keystones.name: Ai-Ais Hot Springs
keystones.description: A desert national park in southern Namibia's Fish River Canyon region, famed for the natural hot springs at Ai-Ais and the dramatic Fish River Canyon - Africa's largest canyon. The park forms part of the Ai-Ais/Richtersveld Transfrontier Park with South Africa and supports desert-adapted wildlife including Hartmann's mountain zebra and klipspringer.
keystones.manager: Namibia Ministry of Environment and Tourism

keystones.name: Bwabwata
keystones.description: A national park in the Caprivi Strip of northeastern Namibia, protecting Kavango and Zambezi floodplain ecosystems within the Kavango-Zambezi Transfrontier Conservation Area. The park supports African wild dogs, elephants, hippos, and exceptional birdlife, and is managed with local Khwe San communities who live within its boundaries.
keystones.manager: Namibia Ministry of Environment and Tourism

keystones.name: Etosha
keystones.description: Built around an immense mineral pan visible from space, Etosha supports over 100 mammal species including black rhino, lion, and four giraffe subspecies, all drawn to its iconic waterholes in one of the most productive wildlife viewing areas in Africa.

keystones.name: Namib-Naukluft
keystones.description: One of the world's largest national parks, protecting the ancient Namib Desert and the Naukluft Mountains along Namibia's Atlantic coast. The park encompasses the iconic red dunes of Sossusvlei, Sandwich Harbour lagoon, and extraordinary desert-adapted life including oryx, springbok, brown hyenas, and endemic Welwitschia plants.
keystones.manager: Namibia Ministry of Environment and Tourism

keystones.name: Skeleton Coast
keystones.description: A hauntingly beautiful wilderness where the Namib Desert meets the cold Atlantic, supporting desert-adapted lions, brown hyena, and vast seal colonies in one of the most extreme and least accessible landscapes in Africa.

keystones.name: Termit et Tin-Toumma
keystones.description: One of Africa's largest protected areas, safeguarding Saharan and Sahelian habitats in eastern Niger. The reserve is a critical refuge for the world's last wild population of addax antelope, alongside dorcas gazelles, Saharan cheetahs, and Barbary sheep in a remote and largely pristine desert landscape.

keystones.name: Cross River
keystones.description: Nigeria's most biodiverse national park, protecting Upper Guinea and Congo Basin transitional rainforest in southeastern Nigeria. Cross River is the primary refuge for the critically endangered Cross River gorilla, with fewer than 300 individuals remaining, and connects with Cameroon's Takamanda National Park across the border.

keystones.name: Gashaka-Gumti
keystones.description: Nigeria's largest national park, encompassing forest, savanna, and montane habitats on the Cameroon highland border, including Chappal Waddi - Nigeria's highest peak. The park supports chimpanzees, lions, African wild dogs, and diverse antelope in a landscape that spans multiple ecological zones.

keystones.name: Kainji Lake
keystones.description: A national park in northwestern Nigeria centred on Lake Kainji reservoir on the Niger River, protecting Guinea savanna and riparian habitats. The park harbours lions, leopards, elephants, and hippos, and provides critical ecosystem services including water regulation for one of West Africa's largest artificial lakes.

keystones.name: Yankari
keystones.description: Nigeria most important wildlife sanctuary and home to West Africa largest elephant population, protecting Guinea-Sudanian savanna around the warm Wikki Spring in one of the few remaining natural landscapes in the most populous nation in Africa.

keystones.name: Akagera
keystones.description: Central Africa largest protected wetland and Rwanda savannah refuge, transformed by African Parks from a degraded reserve to a thriving Big Five destination including the first wild rhinos to return to Rwanda in decades.

keystones.name: Nyungwe
keystones.description: The largest montane rainforest remaining in East or Central Africa, home to 13 primate species including chimpanzees and the rare Albertine rift endemics, and a critical water catchment for both the Nile and Congo river systems.

keystones.name: Volcans
keystones.description: The Rwandan sector of the Virunga Massif and home to approximately one third of the world remaining mountain gorillas, one of the most important wildlife sanctuaries on Earth and a globally significant conservation success story.

keystones.name: Dinder
keystones.description: Sudan's largest national park and a UNESCO biosphere reserve on the Ethiopian border, protecting Sahelian savanna and the seasonal Dinder and Rahad river systems. The park supports large-scale wildlife migrations including tiang, roan antelope, and reedbuck, with significant lion, leopard, and wild dog populations.

keystones.name: Niokolo-Koba
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site in southeastern Senegal and the most important protected area in West Africa for large mammal conservation, sheltering the largest remaining population of western giant eland alongside lion, leopard, and chimpanzee.

keystones.name: Badingilo
keystones.description: The northern terminus of the Great Nile Migration and a vast floodplain savanna managed by African Parks since 2022, protecting the seasonal movement of millions of antelope across some of the most biodiverse grasslands remaining in Africa.

keystones.name: Boma
keystones.description: One of the largest protected areas in Africa and the terminus of the Great Nile Migration - the largest land mammal migration on Earth - with over six million white-eared kob, tiang, and Mongalla gazelle crossing between Boma and Ethiopia Gambella.

keystones.name: Southern
keystones.description: South Sudan's largest national park and one of Africa's most significant yet least-known wildlife areas, protecting vast floodplains and savanna in the Sudd basin. The park encompasses the world's second-largest mammal migration, with millions of white-eared kob, tiang, and Mongalla gazelle moving seasonally across its plains.

keystones.name: Zeraf
keystones.description: A vast wetland complex in the Sudd - the world largest tropical wetland - supporting enormous concentrations of waterfowl and forming a critical hydrological buffer that sustains biodiversity across the Nile Basin.

keystones.name: Aouk
keystones.description: A national park in southeastern Chad protecting Sudan-Guinea savanna and gallery forest along the Aouk River on the Central African Republic border. Part of the broader Zakouma-Aouk landscape, the park forms a corridor for elephants and other migratory wildlife moving between protected areas across this region.

keystones.name: Ennedi
keystones.description: A vast sandstone massif in northeast Chad, sculpted into extraordinary canyons and natural arches, sheltering desert-adapted wildlife including Barbary sheep and the last wild camels in the Sahara.

keystones.name: Ouadi Rime-Ouadi Achim
keystones.description: One of Africa's largest protected areas, a vast Sahelian game reserve in central Chad that was once the stronghold of the scimitar-horned oryx before its extinction in the wild. International reintroduction efforts are now returning oryx to this sweeping desert-steppe landscape of dry wadis and Saharan grasslands.

keystones.name: Zakouma
keystones.description: A remarkable conservation success story where elephant populations have recovered from near-collapse to over 700 individuals under African Parks management since 2010, making it one of the most secure large mammal strongholds in Central Africa.

keystones.name: Fazao-Malfakassa
keystones.description: Togo's largest national park, protecting Guinea savanna, rocky outcrops, and gallery forest in the country's centre. The park supports elephants, buffalos, and diverse primate communities, and forms a critical wildlife area in one of West Africa's most densely populated regions where natural habitat is increasingly fragmented.

keystones.name: Katavi
keystones.description: One of Tanzania most remote and least visited parks, protecting a spectacular floodplain ecosystem on the Katuma River where thousands of hippo and crocodile compete for space in shrinking pools during the dry season.

keystones.name: Kilimanjaro
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage site protecting Africa's highest mountain at 5,895m and the world's largest freestanding volcanic peak. The park's ecological zones rise from montane forest through moorland and alpine desert to the glaciated summit, forming a globally iconic landscape and critical water tower for northern Tanzania and Kenya.

keystones.name: Kilombero
keystones.description: A vast game-controlled area protecting the Kilombero Valley floodplain in south-central Tanzania, one of Africa's most important freshwater wetlands and a Ramsar site. The valley's seasonal flooding supports enormous herds of puku antelope, hippos, crocodiles, and the globally threatened wattled crane.

keystones.name: Kitulo Plateau
keystones.description: A high-altitude national park in the Southern Highlands of Tanzania, often called the Serengeti of Flowers for its extraordinary seasonal displays of wild orchids, aloes, and mountain flowers. The plateau supports endemic lizards and birds, and is a critical breeding ground for the endangered Kipunji monkey.

keystones.name: Kitwai
keystones.description: A game-controlled area in northern Tanzania between Tarangire and the Masaai Steppe, forming a critical dry-season dispersal area for elephants and wildebeest from Tarangire National Park. The area's intact connectivity allows one of East Africa's last great seasonal wildlife migrations to continue across unfenced landscape.

keystones.name: Luasi
keystones.description: A game reserve in western Tanzania protecting miombo woodland and connecting habitats between Katavi and Mahale Mountains national parks. The reserve forms a vital corridor for elephants and other large mammals moving between two of Tanzania's most remote and pristine protected areas.

keystones.name: Luganzo-Tongwe
keystones.description: A game reserve in western Tanzania protecting forest and woodland habitats adjacent to Mahale Mountains National Park on Lake Tanganyika. The area provides critical buffer and corridor habitat for chimpanzees and other forest species in one of Tanzania's most biologically rich but least-studied western landscapes.

keystones.name: Mahale Mountains
keystones.description: A remote national park on the eastern shore of Lake Tanganyika protecting montane forest, miombo, and lakeshore habitats. Mahale is world-famous for its fully habituated wild chimpanzee communities, offering unparalleled observation of chimpanzee behaviour, and is accessible only by boat or small aircraft in one of Africa's most pristine settings.

keystones.name: Mkomazi
keystones.description: A national park in northeastern Tanzania contiguous with Kenya's Tsavo West, protecting semi-arid Acacia-Commiphora savanna and the Pare and Usambara mountain foothills. Mkomazi is home to a successful black rhino sanctuary and wild dog breeding programme, and forms part of the Tsavo-Mkomazi ecosystem.

keystones.name: Moyowosi
keystones.description: A vast game reserve in western Tanzania protecting the Moyowosi and Malagarasi wetland systems, among Africa's most extensive inland freshwater ecosystems. The area supports shoebills, sitatunga, hippos, and large elephant herds, and connects Gombe and Mahale national parks within a broader western Tanzania conservation landscape.

keystones.name: Mpanga-Kipengere
keystones.description: A game reserve in the Southern Highlands of Tanzania protecting the Kipengere Range and Mpanga Forest, part of the Eastern Afromontane biodiversity hotspot. The reserve supports the endemic Kipunji monkey, the last large primate discovered in Africa, alongside other montane forest specialists.

keystones.name: Ngorongoro
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site built around the world largest intact volcanic caldera, sheltering the densest concentration of large mammals in Africa and one of the most genetically isolated lion populations, alongside the Maasai people who continue to graze their cattle within its boundaries.

keystones.name: Nyerere
keystones.description: Africa's largest national park, carved from the northern section of the former Selous Game Reserve and named after Tanzania's founding president. Nyerere protects an immense miombo woodland and riverine landscape supporting Africa's largest elephant population in a single protected area, alongside lions, wild dogs, and hippos.

keystones.name: Ruaha
keystones.description: Tanzania largest national park and one of the most important elephant refuges in Africa, protecting a vast miombo and acacia woodland ecosystem with some of the highest densities of lion, leopard, cheetah, and wild dog remaining anywhere.

keystones.name: Rungwa
keystones.description: A game reserve in central Tanzania forming part of the Rungwa-Kizigo-Muhesi ecosystem, one of Tanzania's largest miombo woodland conservation areas. The reserve connects Ruaha National Park to the broader central Tanzania ecosystem and supports large elephant herds, wild dogs, and lions in intact woodland.

keystones.name: Saadani
keystones.description: Tanzania's only coastal national park, uniquely placing wildlife on the beach where lions, elephants, and giraffes roam alongside the Indian Ocean. The park protects mangroves, coastal forest, and savanna, with green turtle nesting beaches and dhow sailing traditions forming part of its living cultural landscape.

keystones.name: Selous
keystones.description: The southern portion of what was once Africa's largest protected area and a UNESCO World Heritage site, now designated for photographic tourism and trophy hunting blocks. The remaining Selous Game Reserve adjoins Nyerere National Park and continues to protect vast miombo ecosystems with significant wildlife populations.

keystones.name: Serengeti
keystones.description: The anchor of the greatest wildlife spectacle on Earth - the annual wildebeest migration of over 1.5 million animals - and a UNESCO World Heritage Site protecting the most intact large mammal ecosystem remaining on the planet.

keystones.name: Tarangire
keystones.description: A national park in northern Tanzania's safari circuit, famous for its ancient baobab trees and the largest elephant concentration in the world outside Botswana during the dry season. The Tarangire River is a lifeline for wildlife across a broader ecosystem that includes critical dispersal areas extending to the Masaai Steppe.

keystones.name: Udzungwa Mountains
keystones.description: The largest and most biodiverse block of the Eastern Arc Mountains - Africa Galapagos - sheltering dozens of endemic species found nowhere else on Earth, including the Sanje mangabey and Iringa red colobus, both unique to these ancient forests.

keystones.name: Ugalla
keystones.description: A remote game reserve in western Tanzania protecting miombo woodland along the Ugalla River, one of Tanzania's least-visited but biologically important protected areas. The reserve harbours elephants, lions, African wild dogs, and sable antelope, and connects the western Tanzania conservation landscape.

keystones.name: Uluguru
keystones.description: A forest reserve in the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania, protecting one of the world's highest densities of endemic species per unit area. The Uluguru Mountains' mist forests harbour dozens of endemic chameleons, frogs, birds, and plants found nowhere else on Earth, and supply water to Dar es Salaam's growing population.

keystones.name: Uzungwa Scarp
keystones.description: A forest reserve protecting the dramatic scarp face of the Udzungwa Mountains, part of the Eastern Arc Mountains biodiversity hotspot. The reserve protects the Sanje mangabey and Udzungwa red colobus - primates found nowhere else on Earth - alongside extraordinary levels of plant and amphibian endemism.

keystones.name: Bwindi Impenetrable
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most biodiverse forests in Africa, home to approximately half the world mountain gorilla population and over 350 bird species, protected by the Uganda Wildlife Authority.

keystones.name: Kidepo Valley
keystones.description: A remote and spectacular savanna in Uganda far northeast, sheltering lions, cheetah, and diverse antelope species in an arid landscape that remains one of the least visited and most pristine wilderness areas in East Africa.

keystones.name: Mount Elgon
keystones.description: A national park straddling the Uganda-Kenya border protecting Africa's largest volcanic caldera and the ancient Elgon Forest, a critical water tower for both nations. The park harbours elephants who excavate salt caves for minerals, alongside forest duikers, black-and-white colobus monkeys, and spectacular afromontane flora.

keystones.name: Murchison Falls
keystones.description: Uganda largest national park, bisected by the Nile as it thunders through a narrow gorge in the most powerful waterfall on Earth, supporting large populations of elephant, buffalo, hippo, and the endangered Rothschild giraffe.

keystones.name: Pian Upe
keystones.description: Uganda's largest wildlife reserve, protecting semi-arid savanna and rocky terrain in the Karamoja region of northeastern Uganda. The reserve is home to Uganda's only population of roan antelope, alongside cheetahs, ostriches, and Grevy's zebras in a landscape more akin to Kenya's drylands than Uganda's typical green terrain.

keystones.name: Queen Elizabeth
keystones.description: Uganda's most visited national park, protecting savanna, wetlands, and tropical forest across a landscape that includes the Kazinga Channel linking Lakes Edward and George. The park is famous for tree-climbing lions in the Ishasha sector, vast hippo and buffalo herds, and the Kyambura Gorge chimpanzee community.

keystones.name: Rwenzori Mountains
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage site protecting the Rwenzori range, Ptolemy's legendary Mountains of the Moon straddling the Uganda-DRC border with peaks permanently glaciated above 4,500m. The park's afroalpine zone harbours giant heathers, lobelias, and groundsels of extraordinary scale, with the rare Rwenzori turaco among its endemic birds.

keystones.name: Kalahari Gemsbok
keystones.description: The South African half of the Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park, the first formal transfrontier conservation area in southern Africa. The park's red dunes, dry riverbeds, and blue skies create one of Africa's most dramatic landscapes, with black-maned lions, cheetahs, meerkats, and massive raptor concentrations.

keystones.name: Kruger
keystones.description: South Africa largest and most iconic national park, protecting the Big Five across a vast mosaic of bushveld, river courses, and granite outcrops, and the operational anchor of the Greater Limpopo Transfrontier Conservation Area.

keystones.name: Mountain Zebra-Camdeboo
keystones.description: Two adjacent national parks in the Eastern Cape protecting Karoo shrubland and mountain ecosystems, combined as a conservation unit. Mountain Zebra National Park saved the Cape mountain zebra from extinction and now holds a recovered population, while Camdeboo protects the Valley of Desolation and classic Karoo landscapes.

keystones.name: Namaqua
keystones.description: A national park on South Africa's Namaqualand coast protecting one of the world's most spectacular wildflower displays, when orange and yellow daisies carpet the semi-desert after spring rains. The park encompasses the unique Succulent Karoo biome, one of only two globally recognised biodiversity hotspots that are arid.

keystones.name: Richtersveld
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage site in South Africa's Northern Cape protecting the Succulent Karoo in its most rugged and intact form, where the Orange River meets the Namibian border. The park has the highest plant diversity of any arid region on Earth, with extraordinary succulents and the ancient quiver tree.

keystones.name: Cederberg
keystones.description: A wilderness area in the Western Cape protecting the Cederberg range, whose dramatic sandstone formations and unique fynbos vegetation form one of South Africa's most beloved mountain landscapes. The area is a centre of Cape Floral Kingdom endemism and contains significant San rock art sites among its weathered rock formations.

keystones.name: Bangweulu
keystones.description: A vast community-owned wetland in northeastern Zambia and one of Africa most extraordinary freshwater ecosystems, home to the endemic black lechwe antelope and the rare shoebill stork, managed in partnership with local fishing communities.

keystones.name: Kafue
keystones.description: Zambia largest national park and one of the biggest in Africa, sheltering over 150 mammal species across vast miombo woodland and floodplain ecosystems, with a carbon project co-managed with local communities in the surrounding landscape.

keystones.name: Liuwa Plain
keystones.description: Home to Africa second-largest wildebeest migration and managed since 2003 under a historic partnership between African Parks, the Zambian government, and the Barotse Royal Establishment, making it a pioneering model of community co-governance.

keystones.name: Lower Zambezi
keystones.description: A pristine wilderness along the Zimbabwe border where the Zambezi River defines the park boundary, home to large herds of elephant and buffalo and the site of one of Zambia most successful REDD+ carbon projects.

keystones.name: Lunga Luswishi
keystones.description: A vast game management area in northwestern Zambia, protecting miombo woodland and river systems critical to Kafue connectivity, and the site of a carbon enhancement project generating finance for community-led conservation.

keystones.name: Musalangu
keystones.description: A large game management area in northern Zambia's Luapula Province, protecting miombo woodland and connecting habitats between Zambia's northern parks. The area harbours elephants, lions, and leopards within a broader landscape that links North Luangwa and Lusenga Plain national parks.
keystones.manager: Department of National Parks and Wildlife Zambia

keystones.name: Mweru Wantipa
keystones.description: A national park in northern Zambia on the shores of Lake Mweru Wantipa, protecting wetland and miombo woodland habitats in one of Zambia's least-visited conservation areas. The park's lake and surrounding marshes support hippos, crocodiles, and significant waterbird concentrations including shoebills.
keystones.manager: Department of National Parks and Wildlife Zambia

keystones.name: North Luangwa
keystones.description: A remote and barely visited wilderness north of South Luangwa, managing one of Africa most successful black rhino reintroduction programmes through the Frankfurt Zoological Society in partnership with the Zambian Wildlife Authority.

keystones.name: Nsumbu
keystones.description: A national park on the southern shores of Lake Tanganyika in northern Zambia, protecting one of the world's oldest and most biodiverse lakes alongside miombo woodland. The park's clear waters harbour hundreds of endemic cichlid fish species, while its shores support elephants, hippos, and lions in an increasingly remote wilderness.
keystones.manager: Department of National Parks and Wildlife Zambia

keystones.name: South Luangwa
keystones.description: One of Africa great wildlife sanctuaries and the birthplace of the walking safari, protecting a lush floodplain ecosystem on the Luangwa River with exceptional concentrations of leopard, hippo, crocodile, and over 400 bird species.

keystones.name: Chimanimani
keystones.description: A compact but spectacular national park in eastern Zimbabwe protecting the Chimanimani Mountains, a quartzite massif of great botanical significance on the Mozambique border. The park's diverse habitats from river gorges to high plateaux support sable antelope, leopards, and exceptionally high plant endemism within the Eastern Highlands.
keystones.manager: Zimbabwe Parks and Wildlife Management Authority

keystones.name: Chizarira
keystones.description: Zimbabwe's most remote national park, perched on the Zambezi escarpment with dramatic gorges and miombo woodland overlooking Lake Kariba. Chizarira protects significant populations of elephants, lions, leopards, and the endangered black rhino in a wilderness that sees very few visitors, preserving its wild character.
keystones.manager: Zimbabwe Parks and Wildlife Management Authority

keystones.name: Gonarezhou
keystones.description: The Place of Many Elephants in the Shona language - a remote wilderness in southeastern Zimbabwe managed by the Gonarezhou Conservation Trust with Frankfurt Zoological Society, and part of the Great Limpopo Transfrontier Park.

keystones.name: Hwange
keystones.description: Zimbabwe largest national park and home to one of Africa biggest elephant populations - over 45,000 individuals - in a vast Kalahari sand and teak forest ecosystem that supports exceptional wildlife diversity including wild dog and sable antelope.

keystones.name: Mana Pools
keystones.description: A UNESCO World Heritage site on the Zambezi River floodplain in northern Zimbabwe, famous for elephants that stand on their hind legs to reach winter thorn pods and for exceptional predator-prey interactions. Mana Pools allows walking safaris without a guide, creating one of Africa's most elemental wildlife experiences in a landscape of pools, riverine forest, and baobabs.
keystones.manager: Zimbabwe Parks and Wildlife Management Authority

keystones.name: Matetsi
keystones.description: A safari area in northwestern Zimbabwe between Hwange National Park and Victoria Falls, forming a critical part of the Kavango-Zambezi Transfrontier Conservation Area. The area supports significant wildlife including the world's largest free-roaming elephant population as part of the broader Kavango-Zambezi ecosystem.

keystones.name: Matusadona
keystones.description: A rugged wilderness on the shores of Lake Kariba in Zimbabwe, managed by African Parks since 2019 and the site of a landmark rhino relocation programme returning black rhinos to their historic range for the first time in decades.
